description: From a concrete example, we may infer that something exists.

guides.text: The goal is $P(a) \to \exists x.\; P(x)$. If $a$ satisfies $P$, then there exists *something* that satisfies $P$. This is the principle of [existential-generalisation|existential generalisation] — from a concrete [witness|witness], you can claim existence.
Start by assuming the concrete instance: $P(a)$.

The rule `exists-intro` takes three arguments: a step number, the concrete term you're generalising from, and a fresh variable name. Here, replace the term `a` with the binding variable `x` to get $\exists x.\; P(x)$. The [context|context] is unchanged — we haven't assumed anything about $x$.
[assumption|Discharge] $P(a)$ to get the full [implication|implication]. From any specific $a$ that is $P$, existence follows.

unlocks.name: ∃-intro
unlocks.description: If you have a concrete example $\varphi$ involving term $t$, generalise it to $\exists x.\; \varphi$ by replacing $t$ with a fresh variable $x$. From the particular to the existential.
